Chavez describes the agreement as an effort to promote continental unity and help countries reduce their fuel bills at a time of skyrocketing oil prices.
(Through Translator) PetroCaribe is a platform for the integration of Latin America and the Caribbean.
Under the agreement, Venezuela will provide lower energy prices by selling directly to Caribbean nations, eliminating intermediaries.
The agreement also allows countries to finance 40 percent of their purchases at only 1 percent interest for 25 years.
PetroCaribe will supply close to 190,000 barrels per day of oil and fuel to countries like Jamaica, the Dominican Republic and Grenada.
The deal also includes a barter provision that allows countries to pay fuel bills with products like bananas or sugar.
(Spanish spoken)
Jose Toro Hardy is a former director of Venezuela's state oil company and an ardent critic of Chavez.
Toro Hardy says that Venezuela can only afford to be so generous given high oil prices.
(Through Translator) A fall in oil prices combined with the PetroCaribe agreement will leave the Venezuelan government in a very difficult position.
Others say the real purpose of PetroCaribe is for Chavez to expand his political influence in the region.
Fidel Castro is one of the biggest beneficiaries of Venezuela's energy cooperation.
Cuba receives some 90,000 barrels of oil per day from Venezuela.
PetroCaribe also comes as Chavez is presenting his leftist agenda as an alternative to the traditional US domination of the region.
With Chavez's approval ratings close to 70 percent, it's not hard to find people in Venezuela who think PetroCaribe is a good idea.
Mr. MIGUEL VALENCIA(ph) (Taxi Driver): (Spanish spoken)
Taxi driver Miguel Valencia refuels his 1981 Buick Century for about 25 cents a gallon.
He believes others should have cheap fuel like Venezuelan drivers.
For NPR News, I'm Brian Ellsworth in Caracas.
Stay with us on DAY TO DAY from NPR News.
Just when you thought Tony Soprano had left the scene, he raises his Bada Bada Bing head, this time for a charity.
This week, 25 outfits worn by James Gandolfini in the Sopranos were auctioned off to benefit the Wounded Warrior Project, a group that aids troops wounded in Iraq and Afghanistan.

The high bid did not go for Tony's signature bathrobe or the black and beige polo shirt, but instead for the shirt, tank top and pants that Tony was wearing when he was shot by the demented Uncle Junior.
Mr. Gandolfini who attended the event at Christie's in New York along with one of the wounded soldiers helped bring in over 187,000 dollars for the organization.
Don't stop believing.

Their shadows somewhere in the night.
Don't stop...
Mao Zedong urged his followers to do many things.
Mostly just to be quiet and listen to him.
Certainly, he never said have a sense of humor.
And this week, a French automaker learned the hard way.
Citroen pulled ads from several Spanish newspapers that showed Chairman Mao scowling over a new of their car.
The text read: It's true, we are leaders, but at Citroen the revolution never stops.
We are once more going to put in motion all the machinery of our technological ability in order to repeat in 2008 the successes obtained in previous years.
Despite that greatly forward ad rhetoric, Chinese officials were reportedly displeased.
Chinese Daily Global Times called the ad wantonly distorted.
Citroen lags well behind Volkswagen, GM, Ford and a whole host of Japanese car makers in selling cars in China.
we repeat our good feelings toward the Chinese people and confirm that we respect the representatives and symbols of the country.
His hero was John Simpson, an intrepid reporter for the BBC who for 30 years used gumshoe journalism to sneak into the Taliban's Afghanistan or report from Bosnia.
So when Willem Marx heard that an American company needed summer interns as reporters in Iraq, the young Brit jumped at the chance.
But this was no ordinary reporting assignment.
In fact, it turns out most of the news wasn't quite news.
Willem Marx joins us now.
He wrote an article in Harper's magazine this month titled, Misinformation Intern: My Summer as a Military Propagandist in Iraq.
He joins us by phone from NYU's journalism school in New York, where he's a student.
Nice to have you on the program today.
Nice to be on the show.
Yeah.
You write about riding around Baghdad with a 9mm pistol in your front pocket and $3 million in cash in your trunk.
Is that what you thought of when you applied for an internship in Baghdad?
No, not exactly.
You understand it was a conflict zone and people carry guns.
But that certainly wasn't something I expected to do.
And having that amount of cash in the back of your trunk is also something no 22 year old expects to experience.
How did you go from Oxford University in England to Baghdad?
How did you get there?
Well, physically I flew over to D.C.
I had to pick up a military contractor card so that I had access to all areas, as it were, around Iraq, and then flew via Paris and Amman in Jordan into Baghdad.
And this was through arrangement with a company that came to be known as the Lincoln Group.
Yes.
It was, I think, by that stage known as the Lincoln Group.
I had applied when it was known as Iraq-X.
And what did you think you were getting into?
I wasn't really too sure.
I wasn't entirely naive.
I understood that there were private contractors working in Iraq.
But I had seen a position advertised as a media intern which would involve pitching story ideas and working with Iraqi journalists, primarily based in Baghdad.
That's Flora Lichtman, our video producer.

In Pennsylvania's 18th Congressional District, Democrat Conor Lamb appears to have won a nail-biter special election over Republican Rick Saccone.
That's based on a count of the vote from Pittsburgh member station WESA.
It's a remarkable upset in a district that President Trump won by more than 20 points less than two years ago.
NPR political editor Domenico Montanaro is here to tell us what this result says about the national political climate ahead of November's midterms.
Hi, Domenico.
Hey, Ari.
